segments.s024-g57: 'کجا' is the interrogative/adverb 'where' (placed at the front for a rhetorical/location question). The sequence 'نامور دختری خوبروی' is a poetic adjective-noun ordering: 'نامور' (renowned) and 'خوبروی' (fair-faced) both modify 'دختری' ('a/one maiden'); 'دختری' is indefinite here. The phrase functions as the subject/topic of the clause that follows.
segments.s024-g58: 'به پرده درون' is a locative phrase meaning 'within the veil/curtain' (literally 'to/into the curtain inside') with 'به' introducing the location and 'درون' specifying 'inside'. 'بود' is the past tense copula 'was', linking back to the preceding noun phrase (the maiden). 'بی‌گفت‌گوی' is an adjectival/advective description 'silent / taciturn', characterizing her; word order (locative + بود + predicate adjective) is typical in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s024-g59: 'پرستنده' is a noun 'admirer/devotee' (one who worships or adores). 'کردیش' is a contracted possessive/ethnic marker here meaning 'her Kurd' or 'her Kurdish (one)' (classical Persian contraction of 'کردی' + 'ش'), so together 'پرستنده کردیش' = 'her Kurdish admirer'. 'بر پیش خویش' is an idiomatic locative phrase 'before/in the presence of her / at her side' with 'بر' + 'پیش' + 'خویش' (possessive) indicating position relative to her.
segments.s024-g60: The correlative negation 'نه ... و نه ...' = 'neither ... nor ...' negates the manner in which this was done. 'بر رسم دین' literally 'upon the custom/rite of religion' and the second half repeats the structure with 'کیش' (creed/sect); here 'بر' signals 'according to / in accordance with' and 'رسم' means 'custom/rite'. The whole clause says this relationship was not in line with religious or customary rites.
segments.s024-g1: چنان بد که is a causative/adversative clause meaning “so/so bad that…” (چنان = “so/such”, بد = “bad”), introducing the result clause. هر شب دو مرد جوان is a temporal phrase plus subject: “each night, two young men,” with the time word هر شب before the noun phrase.
segments.s024-g2: چه ... چه ... is the correlative “whether ... or ...” construction; کهتر here means “inferior/younger” (i.e. lesser in rank) and از تخمه پهلوان literally “from the seed/lineage of the champions” = “of the hero’s stock.” The whole phrase qualifies the two young men as either lowly or of noble heroic descent.
segments.s024-g3: خورشگر is an agent noun (خورش + گر) meaning “the stew-maker / cook.” ببردی is the older 2nd-person past form of بردن (“you carried / you took”), and به ایوان شاه is a prepositional phrase “into/to the king’s iwan/porch,” with شاه postposed as possessor.
segments.s024-g4: همی before the verb is an archaic/progressive adverb indicating continued or habitual action (“was/so did”), and ساختی is 2nd-person past “you made/constructed.” راه درمان شاه is a noun phrase “the way/method of cure for the king” (راه = method/way; درمان = remedy/cure; شاه = king).
segments.s024-g5: بکشتی is the past 2nd-person of کشتن (“you killed”). و simply links clauses (“and”). مغزش is مغز + ضمیر -ش, “its brain.” بپرداختی is past 2nd-person of پرداختن; here it means “you attended to / dealt with” (i.e., took out or prepared the brain).
segments.s024-g6: مر is an archaic clitic/pronoun meaning “to me/for me.” آن اژدها را is the direct object marked with را: “that dragon.” خورش ساختی is “you made a stew” (خورش = stew, ساختی = you made). The whole clause says the dragon was turned into a stew for the speaker.
segments.s024-g7: دو پاکیزه uses پاکیزه (“clean/pure/spotless”) substantively with the numeral دو: “two spotless (ones).” از introduces the source/possession: از گوهر پادشا = “from the king’s gems” (گوهر = gem/jewel, پادشا = king), a poetic metaphor for prized persons.
segments.s024-g8: دو مرد is “two men.” گرانمایه is an epithet meaning “esteemed/precious,” and و پارسا adds “and pious/devout,” so the phrase apposes/identifies the two spotless ones as “two esteemed and pious men.”
segments.s024-g9: This is an elliptical nominal clause meaning 'one (of them) had the name Armayil Pak-din.' 'یکی' (a certain one) introduces an individual; 'نام' is the noun 'name' placed before the name (an older/free word order); 'ارمایل' is the proper name; 'پاک‌دین' is an epithet attached after the name meaning 'pure of faith' (an adjectival epithet). The copula 'was' is omitted, as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s024-g10: Parallel to the previous segment: 'دگر' = 'the other/another'; 'نام' again introduces the name; 'گرمایل' is the proper name; 'پیشبین' is an epithet meaning 'seer' or 'one who foresees.' As before the copula is omitted and the sequence reads 'the other was named...'.
segments.s024-g11: 'چنان بد که بودند' = 'they were so bad that ...' — 'چنان' ('so') modifies 'بد' ('bad'), and 'که' is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing the result clause. 'بودند' is the past plural of 'to be' ('they were'). 'روزی' here means 'one day' (a temporal adverb) and 'به هم' ('to each other' / 'together') prepares for the verb in the next segment. The order is typical of classical Persian where temporal phrase can follow the clause.
segments.s024-g12: 'سخن رفت' is an idiomatic verbal phrase meaning 'a talk took place' or simply 'they spoke.' 'هر گونه' = 'every kind/sort'; 'از بیش و کم' literally 'of more and less' = 'of greater and lesser matters' (i.e., all varieties, major and minor). Word order places the verb phrase before the quantified object phrase, a common arrangement in older Persian.
segments.s024-g13: The preposition 'ز' (classical/poetic form of 'از') introduces the theme 'about/concerning.' 'بیدادگر' is an adjectival epithet meaning 'tyrannical' or 'cruel' modifying 'شاه' (king). The following 'وز لشکرش' is a poetic compressed form equivalent to 'و (و) ز لشکرش' — 'and concerning his army.' In context both phrases are objects of 'سخن رفت' in the previous segment.
segments.s024-g14: The conjunction 'و' = 'and' links the next topic. 'زان' = 'from that/thereof' (i.e., 'about those'), 'رسم‌های' = 'customs' (plural), 'بد' = 'bad.' 'اندر' = 'in/within' and 'خورش' literally means 'stew' or 'fare' but here is used idiomatically/poetically to mean his household, table, or courtly practice — i.e., 'bad customs current in his household/court.' This is an archaic, figurative locution.
segments.s024-g15: 'یکی گفت' = 'one (of them) said.' 'ما را' is the patient/beneficiary phrase meaning 'for us' or indicating the people involved ('we/for us'); 'به خوالیگری' is an instrumental prepositional phrase 'by means of flattery' (classical noun derived from 'خواهش' area — here 'خوالیگری' = flattering/pleasing behavior). The verb that completes the thought follows in the next segment.
segments.s024-g16: 'بباید' is the impersonal/modal auxiliary 'it is necessary' / 'one must' (here 'we must'). It governs the following verbal phrase 'بر شاه رفت آوری' — 'بر شاه' = 'to the king' (direction/goal), and 'رفت آوری' is a compound verbal sequence meaning 'to go (and) bring/appear before' (in older Persian sequences the light verb follows; here it expresses approaching/presenting oneself to the king). Together the phrase means 'we must go to the king' (by the means just stated).
segments.s024-g25: This is a temporal subordinate phrase headed by the particle چو + آمد ('when it came'). به هنگام + verbal noun (خون ریختن) means 'at the time of blood‑shedding'; خون ریختن is a verbal noun (infinitive) with its object first (خون) followed by ریختن. The whole segment sets the time frame for the action that follows.
segments.s024-g26: This is the action clause expressed by an infinitive nominal (آویختن) with the indirect preposition به introducing the target (شیرین روان). شیرین here is a proper name (epithet) and روان is 'spirit/soul' used as an epithet of Shirin; اندر is a locative/old poetic preposition reinforcing 'upon/at'. The infinitive functions as the main action (they clung to/pounced on Shirin), with the subject omitted (typical Persian ellipsis).
segments.s024-g27: از آن marks 'from those' or 'those' as the topical/identified group; روزبانان is the noun 'heralds/announcers' and مردم‌کشان is an attributive compound/apposition meaning 'people‑slayers' (i.e. murderous). The whole phrase identifies the agents/actors.
segments.s024-g28: گرفته is a past participle/verb meaning 'seized' (perfective), followed by the object phrase دو مرد جوان را with را marking the definite/accusative object; کشان is a present participle (verbal adjective) meaning 'dragging' and indicates the ongoing manner of the action. Together they read as 'having seized two young men, dragging (them)'.
segments.s024-g29: زنان is the subject 'the women'; پیش + خوالیگران here means 'toward/in front of the khavaliqān' (prepositional phrase indicating direction or target) and تاختند is the simple past plural 'they rushed/attacked'. Word order is subject — directional phrase — verb, a common Persian sequence.
segments.s024-g30: ز بالا 'from above' gives the source/position; به روی اندر انداختند is a verb phrase where انداختند 'they threw' takes the phrase به روی ('onto the face/surface') with اندر as a locative/emphatic poetic preposition. The verb is past plural; the construction describes how the women threw the addressees down.
segments.s024-g31: پر از درد is an adjectival phrase 'full of pain' placed before the noun; خوالیگران را جگر literally 'the khavaliqān's jigar' (jigar = liver used idiomatically as 'heart/seat of feeling') with را marking and emphasizing the affected noun. Poetic inversion places the modifier before the possessed noun, yielding 'the khavaliqān's heart full of pain.'
segments.s024-g32: This contains two parallel nominal phrases in poetic (inverted) order: پر از خون دو دیده ('two eyes full of blood') and پر از کینه سر ('head full of hatred'). Each پر از + noun is an adjectival phrase placed before the body part it qualifies; there is no explicit verb — they function as verbless predicates describing the same group.
segments.s024-g17: 'و' is the simple conjunction 'and'. 'زان پس' is a poetic temporal phrase meaning 'after that' ('زان' = 'from that', 'پس' = 'after'). 'یکی' is an indefinite subject 'someone/a certain one'. 'چاره‌ای' is the object 'a remedy/plan' with the indefinite suffix '-ای'. 'ساختن' appears here as the narrative action 'made/devised' (classical/epic usage treats the infinitive form as the main past action), so the clause reads 'someone devised a plan.'
segments.s024-g18: 'ز' (poetic variant of 'از') is the preposition 'from'/'of'. 'هر گونه' is the adnominal phrase 'every kind/sort' ('هر' = every, 'گونه' = kind/manner). 'اندیشه' is 'thought/intention'. 'انداختن' here uses its sense 'to throw/cast' in the idiom 'انداختن اندیشه' = 'to cast away/drop a thought' (i.e. to make people put aside those thoughts). The whole segment functions as an infinitival purpose/description: 'to cast away every kind of thought.'
segments.s024-g19: 'مگر' introduces an exception 'except/unless'. 'زین دو تن را' refers to 'these two persons/bodies' (archaic demonstrative; the provided lexeme list does not give a matching sense for 'زین', so it is an older demonstrative here). 'را' is the definite object marker. The relative 'که' introduces a clause describing them: 'که ریزند خون' — 'whose blood is (would be) shed'; 'ریزند' is an archaic/poetic form related to ریختن ('to pour/shed'), used here with 'خون' to mean 'to shed blood.'
segments.s024-g20: The structure places the object 'یکی را' ('one' with object marker 'را') before the modal 'توان' + infinitive 'آوریدن' = 'to be able to bring'. 'توان' is the modal 'can/able', and 'آوریدن' is the infinitive 'to bring'. 'برون' is the adverb 'out/forth' modifying the verb phrase. Together: 'to be able to bring out one (of them).'
segments.s024-g21: 'برفتند' is 3rd-person plural past 'they went/departed'. The conjunction 'و' links clauses. 'خوالیگری' is a noun meaning 'soliciting desire/temptation' or 'flirtation/enticing behaviour' and 'ساختند' is 3rd-person plural past 'they made/contrived'. So the clause says 'they went and contrived (or set up) a temptation/enticing scheme.'
segments.s024-g22: 'خورش‌ها' (plural) names the dishes/stews. The conjunction 'و' links the next phrase. 'اندازه' here means 'measure/appropriate amount' and 'بشناختند' is the past tense 'they came to know/they learned/recognized' (the prefix 'بـ-' plus شناختند). Together: 'they prepared the stews and learned the right measure.'
segments.s024-g23: This is a genitive/compound phrase: 'خورش خانه' = 'stew-house/kitchen', followed by 'پادشاه جهان' = 'the king of the world' (a grand epithet). Together they denote 'the royal kitchen' or 'the king's kitchen/dishes.' Word order is head ('خورش') + house + possessor.
segments.s024-g24: 'گرفت' is the past transitive 'seized/took'. The following 'آن دو' ('those two') is the (implicit) object, described by the appositive 'بیدار دل' = 'wakeful/alert‑hearted'. 'در نهان' is an adverbial phrase 'in secret/secretly' modifying the action. So: 'It seized those two, wakeful‑hearted, in secret.' (i.e. the food secretly captivated them.)
segments.s024-g33: همی is an archaic/prosodic adverb here meaning 'continually' (marks habitual or ongoing action). بنگرید is the present/imperfective form of نگر (with the b- present-prefix) and so reads as 'kept looking' or 'they looked repeatedly'. The short sequence این بدان آن بدین is a poetic pair of directional phrases ('this to that, that to this') built with pronominal elements and the prepositional/prefixal b- to show motion/attention back and forth; word order is stylized for rhythm rather than strict modern syntax.
segments.s024-g34: ز is the poetic preposition 'because of' or 'from' introducing the reason. کردار is the noun 'deed, action' and بیداد is a heavy, poetic noun meaning 'injustice/oppression'; together they form the genitive/causal phrase 'because of the oppressive deeds'. شاه زمین is a compound noun phrase 'the king of the land' (شاه = king, زمین = land/country).
segments.s024-g35: از آن دو 'from those two' introduces the source/set; یکی را is the definite object ('one of them') marked by the object marker را. بپرداختند is a past plural verb meaning 'they attended to/dealt with' (here 'took care of / handled one of the two'). Word order is typical older Persian: source + object + verb.
segments.s024-g36: جز این چاره‌ای 'except this one remedy' expresses exclusion (جز = except). نیز here is an emphatic/continuative adverb ('even/also' but functioning to stress the negative). نشناختند is the past negative 'they did not know/recognize' (classical past tense, 3rd person plural), so the clause means 'they knew no other remedy.'
segments.s024-g37: برون کرد is a verb phrase where برون (forth/out) combines with کرد (did/made) to yield 'took out / drew out'. مغز سر گوسفند is the direct object in noun + noun order: 'the brain of the sheep' (مغز = brain, سر = head, گوسفند = sheep). This is straightforward older Persian word order: verb after object phrase is acceptable in poetic narrative.
segments.s024-g38: بیامیخت is a past-tense verb 'he mingled / he mixed' (bi- as an older verbal prefix used with the root آمیز). با introduces the instrument/associate 'with'. مغز آن ارجمند is the noun phrase 'the brain of that noble one' (آن = that; ارجمند = esteemed/noble). The clause therefore describes mixing the sheep's brain with that esteemed brain.
segments.s024-g39: یکی را به جان داد is an idiomatic past action: one (یکی را) is the object (marked by را) and به جان داد literally 'gave to life' i.e. 'spared' or 'gave life to' — داد is the past 'gave'. زنهار is an interjection 'beware!' or 'take heed', and و گفت 'and he said' introduces reported speech. The sequence is compact, typical of epic diction.
segments.s024-g40: نگر is the imperative 'look!' or 'see!' addressing the addressee. تا here has a purposive/temporal sense 'so that/until' introducing the clause of result or purpose. بیاری is a subjunctive/imperative verbal form meaning 'you bring' (classical morphology), followed by سر اندر نهفت 'hide the head within' where سر = head, اندر = inside/within, نهفت = concealed/put away. The whole line is an instruction: 'Look, so that you bring it and hide the head inside.'
segments.s024-g41: نگر is an imperative warning ('look!, beware'); تا introduces a subordinate clause expressing the undesired state ('that/so that'); نباشی is the negative 2nd‑person subjunctive/jussive meaning 'you not be' (a prohibition/hope). The prepositional phrase به آباد شهر ('in/into the inhabited/prosperous city') follows the verb phrase and gives the place to be avoided.
segments.s024-g42: تو را is a topicalized/dative marking of 'you' (here putting the person in focus); از جهان introduces the domain 'from the world' or 'as for the world'; the nominal phrase دشت و کوه ('plain and mountain') is the predicative content and است is the copula 'is'. بهر at the end functions as 'for' — the whole clause means 'for you, the world is plain and mountain.' The word order (topic — domain — predicate — copula — beneficiary) is archaic/poetic.
segments.s024-g43: به جای is an idiom meaning 'in place of' or 'instead of'; سرش = 'his head' (سر + enclitic pronoun ش = 'his'); زان ('from/that') points to 'that' item; سری here is 'head' (noun) and بی‌بها is an adjective 'priceless/invaluable' modifying سری. The whole noun phrase means 'that priceless head in place of his head.'
segments.s024-g44: خورش ساختند is a simple past action 'they made/prepared a stew' (ساختند = 3rd‑person plural past 'they made'); از پی اژدها is a prepositional phrase where از marks source/ingredient and پی + noun here indicates 'from/after/coming from the dragon' — poetically 'from the dragon.' Together: 'they made a stew from the dragon.'
segments.s024-g45: از این‌گونه = 'of/from these kinds' (این‌گونه = 'such/these kinds'); هر attaches to ماهیان to mean 'each (of the) fish(es)' (classical usage permits 'هر' + plural to mean 'each'); سی جوان gives the quantity and quality: 'thirty young (ones)'. The short noun phrase states 'from each of these kinds of fish, thirty young [are obtained].
segments.s024-g46: از ایشان = 'from them' (referring back to the creatures); همی + یافتندی is a past‑habitual construction (همی + past) meaning 'they used to find' or 'they would find' (یافتندی = 3rd‑person plural past). روان here is a substantive meaning 'living beings/spirits' (poetic/archaic usage) — so 'they would obtain/find thirty living young from them.'
segments.s024-g47: چو = 'when' (introducing a temporal clause); گرد آمدی is a poetic/archaic expression for 'gathered/assembled' (گرد + آمدی; آمدی here functions as a past plural form in classical usage); مرد = 'men'; از ایشان دویست = 'two hundred of them' (از ایشان marks the subset). The clause means 'when two hundred of their men had come together.'
segments.s024-g48: بر آن سان 'in that manner/so' introduces the result clause; که + نشناختندی is a subordinate clause with نشناختندی (past plural 'they did not recognize'); the final relative clause که کیست is an embedded question 'who he is' (کی + ست = 'who is') — together: 'in such a way that they did not know who he was.' Note the archaic order کیست (who+is) rather than modern 'کی است'.
segments.s024-g49: A noun phrase with the agent noun خورشگر ('the herdsman') followed by the dative/benefactive phrase بدیشان ('to them'). The goods given follow: بزی چند و میش — note that چند is a post‑nominal quantifier here meaning 'a few/several', and the coordinate و links 'goats' (بزی) and 'ewes' (میش).
segments.s024-g50: سپردی is a past tense verb 'handed/entrusted' with an implied subject (the herdsman); in epic Persian the past verb often follows the noun phrase. The coordinating و links the two clauses. صحرا نهادند پیش is a small clause where نهادند (past plural 'they placed/set') takes صحرا ('the plain') as its object and پیش ('ahead/before') as an adverbial; the word order is slightly poetic (object before verb and adverb after) meaning 'they set up on the plain ahead'.
segments.s024-g51: کنون کرد is an adverb + past verb 'now he made/did'. از آن تخمه is a prepositional phrase 'from that stock/seed'; دارد نژاد literally 'has/possesses a lineage' — the present tense دارد is used here to state the resulting property (he caused that stock to have a lineage). Overall: 'Now he made from that stock a lineage.'
segments.s024-g52: که introduces a relative clause modifying نژاد ('a lineage which ...'). ز is the poetic form of از ('from'); آباد here means 'the inhabited/settled place' (the civilized land). ناید is a present/gnomic negative 'does not come' (i.e. 'does not reach/enter'). به دل برش یاد is the remainder: به دل 'into the heart', یاد 'memory', and برش is an enclitic referring back to the person affected (here 'to him/for him'); together 'the memory does not come into his heart.' Note: برش is an enclitic pronoun in this construction (not the 'cut' sense of برش).
segments.s024-g53: پس ('thus/then') opens the clause. آیین ضحاک is a genitive construction 'the custom/practice of Zahhak' (noun + name). وارونه‌خوی is a post‑positive adjective ('perverse, contrary‑natured') describing that custom; Persian frequently places adjectives after the noun, especially compounds like this.
segments.s024-g54: چنان بد که is a result/comparative clause 'so bad that'. The verb phrase می‌بدش is an imperfective/presentive form 'feels aversion / dislikes it' with the enclitic object ش ('it'); آرزوی ('desire/wish') appears after the verb as the subject — Persian often allows verb before subject, so می‌بدش آرزوی = 'desire loathed it.' Here چون functions as a linking particle ('that/so that') introducing the subordinate idea.
segments.s024-g55: ز ('from/of', poetic) مردان جنگی is a genitive noun phrase 'the warlike men / warriors' (مردان modified by جنگی). یکی is the indefinite 'one / someone' and precedes the verb in typical Persian word order. خواستی is the past second‑person 'you wished/desired' — here it governs the following action (you wanted one of them for killing).
segments.s024-g56: بکشتی is the past form 'you killed' (or, when combined with the preceding خواستی, functions as the verbal complement meaning 'you wished to kill'). چو here = 'when', and با دیو برخاستی is a temporal clause 'you rose/stood up with/against the demon' (با = 'against/with' in encounter sense, دیو = 'demon', برخاستی = past 'you rose/advanced'). Together: '(you chose) one to kill when you rose to meet the demon.'

sentences.s024-s29: Where was there a famed, fair-faced maiden inside the veil, silent?
sentences.s024-s30: She kept a Kurdish admirer at her side (in her presence), not according to the customs of religion nor according to the customs of creed.
sentences.s024-s1: It was so bad that each night two young men — whether lesser or of the hero’s stock — (were ...).
sentences.s024-s2: You, the cook, carried it into the king’s iwan; you thus fashioned the king’s remedy/means of cure.
sentences.s024-s3: You killed it and removed its brain; for me you made that dragon into a stew.
sentences.s024-s4: Two spotless ones from the king’s jewels — two esteemed and devout men.
sentences.s024-s5: One was named Armayil Pak-din; the other was called Garmayil, the seer.
sentences.s024-s6: They were in such a bad state that one day they spoke together about all sorts of matters, both greater and lesser.
sentences.s024-s7: About the tyrant king and his army, and about those bad customs in his household/court.
sentences.s024-s8: One said, 'By flattery we must go to the king.'
sentences.s024-s13: When the time for bloodshed came, they fell upon the sweet‑spirited Shirin.
sentences.s024-s14: Those murderous heralds had seized two young men and were dragging them.
sentences.s024-s15: The women rushed at the khavaliqān and hurled them down from above onto their faces.
sentences.s024-s16: The khavaliqān's hearts were full of pain, their two eyes full of blood, their heads full of hatred.
sentences.s024-s9: And after that someone devised a plan to cast away every sort of thought.
sentences.s024-s10: Except that one of those two whose blood would be shed could be brought out.
sentences.s024-s11: They went and contrived solicitation; they prepared the stews and learned the proper measure.
sentences.s024-s12: The royal kitchen's stews seized those two wakeful‑hearted men in secret.
sentences.s024-s17: They kept gazing this way and that because of the king of the land's oppressive deeds.
sentences.s024-s18: From those two they dealt with one; they knew no other remedy.
sentences.s024-s19: He took out the brain of a sheep and mixed it with the brain of that noble one.
sentences.s024-s20: He spared one (gave one life), gave a warning and said, 'Look — until you bring (it), hide the head within.'
sentences.s024-s21: Beware lest you remain in a settled city; for you the world is but plain and mountain.
sentences.s024-s22: In place of his head they set that priceless head, and they made a stew from the dragon.
sentences.s024-s23: From each of these kinds of fish they would find thirty young (ones).
sentences.s024-s24: When two hundred men of them had come together, in such a manner that they did not know who he was.
sentences.s024-s25: The herdsman handed them a few goats and ewes, and they made camp on the plain ahead.
sentences.s024-s26: Now from that stock he produced a lineage which does not let the memory of the inhabited land enter the heart.
sentences.s024-s27: Thus Zahhak's custom, perverse in nature, was so bad that even desire loathed it.
sentences.s024-s28: You chose one of the warlike men to kill when you rose to confront the demon.
